topic: Someone receives flowers with no card attached
raw_output:
[story 1]

The delivery arrived precisely at three, a sharp, insistent chime that cut through the late afternoon quiet of the apartment. I moved to the door with a deliberate slowness, my hand hovering over the lock as if expecting a sudden shift in the air pressure. Inside, nestled in a plain brown paper bag, were a dozen deep purple irises. No note. Just the flowers. My knuckles were white where I gripped the doorknob. I stared at the arrangement, the velvety petals seeming too vibrant, too loud for the tense silence that had settled over the room. A tremor ran through my hand before I finally managed to pull the door open, my breath catching in my throat.

[story 2]

It was the silence that was the worst. The package sat on the entryway table, a stark splash of color against the muted tones of the hallway carpet. I circled it twice, my gaze darting from the cellophane wrapping to the stems, searching for any clue, any whisper of intent. My jaw was tight, a muscle twitching near my ear. Every slight rustle of traffic outside sounded amplified, each passing car a potential threat. When I finally picked up the bouquet, the coolness of the stems against my palm felt strangely alien. I didn't look at the recipient's address label; I just wanted it gone, tucked away somewhere dark, because the lack of context felt like an invitation I wasn't prepared to accept.

[story 3]

My stomach felt like a tightly coiled spring. I hadn't expected anything. The knock on the door was unexpected, a jarring intrusion into the carefully constructed solitude I’d been maintaining. When I opened it, the sheer unexpectedness of the offering hit me like a physical blow. A bouquet of sunflowers, bright and aggressively cheerful, lay on the welcome mat. My eyes scanned the street, cataloging every shadow, every unfamiliar face that might be lurking just beyond the frame of my vision. I took the flowers with a stiff grip, my fingers brushing against the thick paper. I didn't speak; I just backed inside, closing the door with a soft, decisive click, the silence of the apartment suddenly feeling suffocatingly loud.
